perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall promptly advise Company and Client in writing if Recipient learns of any unauthorized use or disclosure of the Confidential Information or Application Software by anyone, whether an employee, former employee or agent of Recipient, or others, and shall immediately take all reasonable steps within Recipient's power to stop any unauthorized use or disclosure of the Confidential Information or Application Software by anyone.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall advise Company and Client in writing within 30 days if Recipient learns of any unauthorized use or disclosure of the Confidential Information or Application Software by anyone, whether an employee, former employee or agent of Recipient, or others, and shall take steps within Recipient's power to stop any unauthorized use or disclosure of the Confidential Information or Application Software by anyone when it is practical.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the reporting of unauthorized use or disclosure to 30 days and making the steps to stop disclosure contingent on practicality introduces a contradiction with the requirement of immediate action in cases involving data breaches or security incidents that many laws demand.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes § 817.5681 mandates that companies must notify individuals affected by a security breach involving their personal information without unreasonable delay, and no later than 30 days after determination of the breach. This modification introduces a delay in reporting unauthorized disclosures that is inconsistent with Florida's security breach notification law, especially considering the nature of confidential data involved.

F.S. 817.5681 817.5681 Breach of security concerning confidential personal information in third-party possession; administrative penalties. — (1)(a) Any person who conducts business in this state and maintains computerized data in a system that includes personal information shall provide notice of any breach of the security of the system, following a determination of the breach, to any resident of this state whose unencrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person. The notification shall be made without unreasonable delay, consistent with the legitimate needs of law enforcement, as provided in subsection (3) and paragraph (10)(a), or subject to any measures necessary to determine the presence, nature, and scope of the breach and restore the reasonable integrity of the system. Notification must be made no later than 45 days following the determination of the breach unless otherwise provided in this section. (b) Any person required to make notification under paragraph (a) who fails to do so within 45 days following the determination of a breach or receipt of notice from law enforcement as provided in subsection (3) is liable for an administrative fine not to exceed $500,000, as follows: 1. In the amount of $1,000 for each day the breach goes undisclosed for up to 30 days and, thereafter, $50,000 for each 30-day period or portion thereof for up to 180 days. 2. If notification is not made within 180 days, any person required to make notification under paragraph (a) who fails to do so is subject to an administrative fine of up to $500,000. (c) The administrative sanctions for failure to notify provided in this subsection shall apply per breach and not per individual affected by the breach. (d) The administrative sanctions for failure to notify provided in this subsection shall not apply in the case of personal information in the custody of any governmental agency or subdivision, unless that governmental agency or subdivisi
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a delay in reporting unauthorized disclosures (30 days) and makes corrective actions contingent on practicality. This contradicts Florida Statutes § 817.5681, which mandates notification 'without unreasonable delay' and sets a maximum notification timeframe of 45 days following the determination of a breach. The change weakens the immediacy required by law, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall, upon the earlier occurrence of (i) completion of the tasks assigned to it by Client which require access to the Confidential Information, or (ii) Recipient's termination of employment with Client, return to Client all copies of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall, within 90 days of the earlier occurrence of (i) completion of the tasks assigned to it by Client which require access to the Confidential Information, or (ii) Recipient's termination of employment with Client, return to Client all copies of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the deadline for returning confidential information to 90 days after project completion or termination introduces a conflict. Such a delay could violate regulations that necessitate prompt and secure handling of sensitive data to prevent unauthorized access or disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Misappropriation of Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 addresses the misappropriation of trade secrets. By allowing a 90-day window for the return of confidential information, the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation. The delay makes it easier for trade secrets to be accessed and potentially used without authorization, thus contradicting the spirit and intention of the law.

(2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 90-day delay in returning confidential information, which creates a contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation states that this delay could violate regulations that necessitate prompt and secure handling of sensitive data to prevent unauthorized access or disclosure. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that the delay increases the risk of misappropriation, contradicting the spirit and intention of 18 U.S. Code § 1836. Scraped Law Snippet 1, which references 18 USC 1836, concerns civil proceedings related to the protection of trade secrets. Although the snippet doesn't explicitly define 'prompt', the introduction of a fixed, extended timeframe for returning confidential information weakens the protection against misappropriation that the law intends to provide, making immediate return impossible and potentially facilitating unauthorized access or use during that period.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall be governed by, interpreted in accordance with, and enforced under the laws of the State of Florida.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall be governed by, interpreted in accordance with, and enforced under the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Florida to California could introduce inconsistencies if the contract contains provisions that are valid under Florida law but violate specific California laws. This creates a potential conflict because certain contractual terms that were initially enforceable under Florida law may become unenforceable or illegal under California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code § 432.5 severely restricts the use of non-compete agreements. If the original agreement, valid under Florida law, contains non-compete clauses that would be invalid under California law, then changing the governing law introduces a direct legal contradiction, making the non-compete provision unenforceable in California.

EMPLOYMENT REGULATION AND SUPERVISION [200 - 2699.8] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) PART 1. COMPENSATION [200 - 452] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 3. Privileges and Perquisites [350 - 452] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 3. Contracts and Applications for Employment [430 - 435] ( Article 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 432.5. No employer, or agent, manager, superintendent, or officer thereof, shall require any employee or applicant for employment to agree, in writing, to any term or condition which is known by such employer, or agent, manager, superintendent, or officer thereof to be prohibited by law. (Added by Stats. 1963, Ch. 559.)
perturbation.justification: The modification changes the governing law from Florida to California. The Law Explanation states that California Labor Code § 432.5 restricts non-compete agreements, which may be valid under Florida law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing LAB 432.5, states, 'No employer...shall require any employee or applicant for employment to agree, in writing, to any term or condition which is known by such employer...to be prohibited by law.' Therefore, if the contract contains a non-compete clause valid under Florida law but prohibited under California law, this change introduces a legal contradiction because enforcing the agreement under California law would violate LAB 432.5. This aligns with the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition, where a deadline or requirement violates state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.
